chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
locked_facts: Rolling without slipping condition: v_cm = ωR
Velocity at contact point is zero (instantaneous axis of rotation at contact)
Velocity at top point is 2v_cm (translation + rotation add)
KE of rolling = ½mv²_cm + ½Iω² = ½mv²_cm(1 + k²/R²)
The contact point is the instantaneous axis of rotation
Friction is necessary for rolling but does no work in pure rolling
For pure rolling, the contact point has zero velocity — no relative sliding
truth_statements: In pure rolling the velocity of the contact point relative to the surface is zero
The total kinetic energy of a rolling body is the sum of translational and rotational kinetic energies
The top of a rolling wheel moves at twice the speed of the centre
Static friction enables rolling without slipping but does not dissipate energy
The condition v = ωR connects translational and rotational motion in pure rolling

aha_moment.what_to_show: A rolling wheel with velocity vectors drawn at contact, centre, and top. Contact: v = v_cm - ωR = 0. Centre: v = v_cm. Top: v = v_cm + ωR = 2v_cm. The instantaneous axis at the contact point is highlighted.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three velocity arrows at three points — zero at bottom, v at centre, 2v at top — with the contact point marked as instantaneous pivot
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think every point on the wheel moves at the same speed — seeing zero velocity at the contact point is the breakthrough moment

simulation_states.label: Pure translation
simulation_states.description: A wheel sliding without rotating. Every point has the same velocity v_cm. Contact point slides — kinetic friction acts.
simulation_states.emphasis: Without rotation, the contact point slides — this is NOT rolling

simulation_states.label: Pure rotation
simulation_states.description: Wheel spinning in place (not translating). Contact point moves backward at ωR. Top moves forward at ωR. Centre is stationary.
simulation_states.emphasis: Without translation, the wheel spins but doesn't go anywhere

simulation_states.label: Rolling = translation + rotation
simulation_states.description: Translation and rotation combined: v_cm = ωR. Contact velocity: v_cm - ωR = 0. Top: v_cm + ωR = 2v_cm. The contact is the instantaneous axis.
simulation_states.emphasis: v_contact = 0 is THE condition for rolling without slipping

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts v_cm and ω independently. When v_cm = ωR, 'pure rolling' indicator turns green. Deviating shows slip. Track a point on the rim — it traces a cycloid.
simulation_states.emphasis: Adjust ω until contact velocity = 0. Track a rim point to see the cycloid path.

common_misconceptions.belief: Friction always opposes motion, so it must slow rolling
common_misconceptions.correction: In pure rolling, static friction acts at the contact point which has zero velocity — so friction does zero work and dissipates no energy. It only provides the torque needed to maintain rolling.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show friction arrow at contact but zero displacement → zero work done

common_misconceptions.belief: All points on a rolling wheel have the same speed
common_misconceptions.correction: In rolling, the contact point has zero velocity, the centre has v_cm, and the top has 2v_cm. The speed varies from 0 to 2v_cm depending on position.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show velocity arrows at contact (0), centre (v), and top (2v)
